title: Your first variable
instruction:
Imagine you have a box. You write "name" on the outside of the box, then you put "Alex" inside it. Whenever you need your name, you just look at the box labeled "name" instead of writing "Alex" over and over again.

That box is a variable. A variable is a labeled box that holds a piece of information.

In Go, you create that box like this:

    name := "Alex"

name is the label you put on the box. := means "create this box and put this value inside". "Alex" is what goes in.

Now whenever you write name, Go opens that box and gives you whatever is inside.

Your turn: create a variable called name with your actual name in it. Then print it with fmt.Println(name).
hint: First line: name := "YourName"  (your name goes inside the quotes). Second line: fmt.Println(name)  (no quotes around name this time — you are reading the box, not typing text).

codeChecks.message: Create a name variable like this: name := "YourName"
Print the variable by writing: fmt.Println(name)
Replace the TODO lines with your actual code.

title: One box is not enough
instruction:
Your contacts app does not just store one phone number. It stores your mom, your best friend, your pizza place, your dentist — each one in their own labeled contact.

Variables work the same way. You can have as many as you want. Each one gets its own label and holds its own value.

The name box already exists. Now let's add a city box:

    city := "Chicago"

Then print both, name first, then city. Two boxes, two Println calls, two lines of output.

Put your actual city in there — nobody's judging!
hint: Add city := "YourCity" below the name line. Then add fmt.Println(city) below fmt.Println(name). Each variable gets its own Println.

codeChecks.message: Create a city variable like this: city := "Chicago"
Replace the TODO lines with your actual code.

title: Write once, use anywhere
instruction:
Here is the real reason variables exist. Imagine you are sending the same greeting to 100 people. Without variables you would type the whole message 100 times. If you made a typo, you would fix it 100 times.

With a variable, you write it once. Use it everywhere. Fix it once if something changes.

The greeting box is already created below. Your job is to print it three times — three separate Println calls, all using the same variable name.

Do NOT copy-paste the text. Use the variable each time. That is the whole point.
hint: Write fmt.Println(greeting) three times, each on its own line. Go will open the box and read the value each time.

codeChecks.message: Call fmt.Println(greeting) three separate times, each on its own line.
Replace the TODO line with your actual code.

title: Boxes can be refilled
instruction:
What happens when your friend gets a new phone number? You do not throw away your contacts app and start fresh. You just tap Edit and type the new number. The label stays the same, the value inside changes.

Variables work exactly like that. You can change what is inside the box at any time.

But here is the important rule:

- := creates a new box  (use this only once, the first time)
- = refills an existing box  (use this when you want to change the value)

The mood box below starts as "tired". Your job is to change it to "energized" after the first print, then print it again. You should see tired first, then energized.
hint: After fmt.Println(mood), write: mood = "energized"  (single = only, not :=). Then write fmt.Println(mood) one more time below that.

codeChecks.message: Change the value using a single = like this: mood = "energized"  — not := which would try to create a new box
Replace the TODO lines with your actual code.

title: Build your own contact card
instruction:
Think of this as creating your own contact entry — the kind you would find in someone's phone.

A contact has a first name, a last name, and a hometown. Three separate pieces of information. Three separate boxes.

From scratch, create these three variables:
- firstName — your first name
- lastName — your last name
- hometown — the city you are from

Then print all three, one per line.

You are putting real information in here. firstName holds "Alex", not the word "firstName". Remember the difference!
hint: Three lines with :=, then three lines with Println. Make sure the names match exactly: firstName, lastName, hometown.

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO lines with your actual code.
